ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03301491 covers beef brisket as a food product; ala carte serving context is not separately coded. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP represents beef brisket as a raw/processed cut; the ala carte restaurant serving format is not separately enumerated. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226355009 covers beef brisket as a substance; the specific restaurant ala carte preparation is not separately coded.

serving.natural_language_portion: One ala carte serving of beef brisket is approximately 150 g, representing a single restaurant portion of sliced or braised brisket served without bread or sides.

nutrient_source.title: Beef, brisket, flat half, separable lean and fat, trimmed to 0 inch fat, all grades, cooked, braised — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked braised beef brisket (flat half, lean and fat, 0-inch trim, all grades) used as the primary nutrient reference. Values represent cooked braised brisket per 100 g, which is the closest defensible match to a restaurant ala carte beef brisket portion. Sodium may be higher in restaurant preparations due to seasoning; the USDA value is used as a conservative baseline.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal); University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: Pure cooked beef contains negligible digestible carbohydrate (0 g per 100 g per USDA). Foods with zero or near-zero carbohydrate content have a glycemic index of effectively 0 and are universally classified as low GI. Per University of Sydney GI Database convention and Brand-Miller et al. (2008), protein- and fat-dominant foods with no glycemic carbohydrates are assigned GI = 0 and GL = 0. Following Veritas v2 critical rule for zero-calorie beverages, glycemic_index is set to 1 (the lowest valid positive value) and gi_category to low, consistent with this convention. glycemic_load_per_serving = 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0.

hypertension.guidance: Beef brisket prepared in restaurant settings typically contains 300–400 mg sodium per 100 g from seasoning and braising liquids; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 100 g or less and request low-sodium preparation where possible.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef brisket contains essentially zero digestible carbohydrate and has a glycemic index of 1, making it a suitable protein source for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; however, total caloric intake and saturated fat should be monitored given the moderate fat content of 14.8 g per 100 g.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Beef brisket contains approximately 5.5 g saturated fat and 93 mg cholesterol per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit consumption to small portions (≤100 g) and prefer leaner cuts or trimmed preparations to reduce saturated fat intake in line with AHA dietary guidelines.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Brisket Ala Carte is a single-serving restaurant portion of cooked beef brisket, typically braised or slow-cooked until tender and served without bread, sides, or accompaniments. Beef brisket is a cut taken from the lower chest or breast of the cow, known for its dense connective tissue that breaks down during long, slow cooking to produce a rich, deeply savoury, and slightly gelatinous texture. In the Singapore food-service context, this dish appears on menus at Western-style sandwich and mix-and-match restaurants such as Subway and similar outlets, where it is offered as a standalone protein component. Nutritionally, beef brisket is a high-protein, moderate-fat food with approximately 26 g protein and 14.8 g fat per 100 g, and negligible carbohydrate content, giving it a glycemic index of effectively zero (set to 1 per Veritas v2 rule). The saturated fat content of approximately 5.5 g per 100 g is clinically relevant for cardiovascular and hypertension management. The sodium content in restaurant preparations may exceed the USDA baseline of 336 mg per 100 g due to seasoning and braising sauces. Beef brisket is suitable for low-carbohydrate and ketogenic dietary patterns and is appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes from a glycemic perspective, though portion control is advised for those managing saturated fat intake. It is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those following halal or kosher diets without specific certification.
common_user_queries: What is beef brisket ala carte?
How many calories in beef brisket?
Is beef brisket good for diabetics?
Is beef brisket high in protein?
How much sodium in beef brisket?
What is the GI of beef brisket?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are sourced from USDA SR Legacy for cooked braised beef brisket (flat half, lean and fat, 0-inch trim), which is the closest defensible approved-source match. The ala carte restaurant preparation at Subway Singapore and Funan Mix & Match may differ in seasoning, sodium, and fat trim level. No Singapore-specific HPB Foodo entry for beef brisket ala carte was identified. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. carbohydrate_g is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked braised beef brisket; beef muscle tissue contains no glycemic carbohydrates. sugar_g is 0.0: naturally zero as beef contains no sugars. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0: naturally zero as beef is an animal product containing no dietary fibre. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA; beef brisket contains no vitamin C. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0: confirmed zero per USDA SR Legacy for this cut. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this specific brisket cut. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for this specific brisket cut. 3. glycemic_index is set to 1 (effectively zero) as beef brisket contains no digestible carbohydrates; gi_category is low per University of Sydney GI Database convention for zero-carbohydrate protein foods and the Veritas v2 rule for non-zero GI values. glycemic_load_per_serving is 0.0 as carbohydrate_g is 0. 4. Halal status is not confirmed; Subway Singapore serves halal-certified products at some outlets but this cannot be generalised to all listed restaurants without verification. 5. Sodium value of 336 mg per 100 g reflects the USDA baseline for braised brisket; actual restaurant sodium may be higher due to seasoning sauces. 6. FoodOn URI FOODON_03301491 and FoodEx2 code A0CKP are used as closest parent matches; the ala carte serving format is not separately enumerated in these ontologies. 7. trans_fat_g of 0.5 g per 100 g reflects naturally occurring conjugated linoleic acid (CLA) and vaccenic acid found in ruminant beef fat, as reported by USDA; this is distinct from industrially produced trans fats.
